notes: Non-Uniform Memory Access architecture for scalable enterprise servers; concept now standard in all modern multi-socket x86 servers.
Multiple CPUs sharing common bus, memory, and OS; well understood but limited scalability beyond 4-10 processors in the 1990s.
Multiple independent nodes cooperating via message-passing; used for HA and resource sharing but had software bottleneck issues in 1996.
Intel's economical microprocessor platform that Sequent leveraged for enterprise superservers.
DEC's pioneering cluster technology for VAX systems; referenced as an example of traditional clustering.
IBM's Scalable POWERparallel system for high-performance computing; referenced as a cluster example.
IBM mainframe clustering technology; evolved into Parallel Sysplex which remains in use on modern z-series.
Core enterprise workload type; one of the primary targets for Sequent's NUMA-Q architecture.
Analytical workloads for complex queries; evolved into modern business intelligence and analytics platforms.
Asynchronous Transfer Mode; predicted to deliver cheap infinite bandwidth by 2001, but was superseded by Gigabit Ethernet.
